quote: From the beginning, it is your custom to bestow your alms in all places, and to furnish subsistence to many churches.
sourceTitle: Letter to Pope Soter, quoted by Eusebius in Ecclesiastical History IV.23
notes: Preserved by Eusebius from Dionysius's correspondence.

quote: Lord, if your people still need me, I am ready for the task; your will be done.
sourceTitle: Traditional saying attributed to Saint Liborius
notes: Traditional devotional attribution retained for local feast-day coverage.

quote: Those who love are never tired, since love knows no burden.
sourceTitle: Canossian Sisters profile and sayings collection
notes: April 10 saint-of-the-day quote sourced from Canossian material.

quote: You have made us for yourself, O Lord, and our hearts are restless until they rest in you.
notes: Opening of Augustine's Confessions. Translation from standard English rendering of the Latin.

quote: To one who has faith, no explanation is necessary. To one without faith, no explanation is possible.
notes: Widely attributed to Aquinas but exact locus in his writings is disputed. Reflects his theological method but direct sourcing is weak.

quote: Lord, make me an instrument of your peace.
sourceTitle: Peace Prayer traditionally attributed to St. Francis
notes: This prayer first appeared in a French magazine in 1912 and is not found in Francis's own writings. Universally associated with him in Catholic devotion but historically not his composition.

aliases: St. Thérèse of the Child Jesus
quote: My vocation is love! In the heart of the Church, my Mother, I will be love.
notes: From Thérèse's autobiography, Manuscript B. Translated from French.

quote: Let nothing disturb you, let nothing frighten you. All things are passing; God never changes. Patience obtains all things. Whoever has God lacks nothing. God alone suffices.
sourceTitle: Bookmark prayer found in her breviary
notes: Found written in Teresa's breviary after her death. Translated from Spanish. One of her most widely reproduced texts.

quote: In the evening of life, we will be judged on love alone.
notes: From John's collected Sayings of Light and Love. Translated from Spanish.

quote: Take, Lord, and receive all my liberty, my memory, my understanding, and my entire will.
sourceTitle: Spiritual Exercises, Contemplation to Attain Love
notes: The Suscipe prayer from the Spiritual Exercises. Translated from Spanish/Latin.

quote: I arise today through a mighty strength, the invocation of the Trinity.
notes: The Lorica is traditionally attributed to Patrick but likely dates to the 7th or 8th century. Deeply embedded in Irish Catholic tradition.

sourceTitle: The Rule of Saint Benedict, Chapter 72
notes: From the Rule of St. Benedict. Translated from Latin. Also echoed in Chapter 4.

quote: Be who God meant you to be and you will set the world on fire.
notes: Widely attributed to Catherine but the exact wording is a modern paraphrase. Her Dialogue contains similar themes but this precise phrasing is not directly sourced.

quote: Have patience with all things, but first of all with yourself.
notes: From Francis de Sales's spiritual correspondence. Translated from French.

quote: Private prayer is like straw scattered here and there: if you set it on fire, it makes a lot of little flames. But gather these straws into a bundle and light them, and you get a mighty fire.
sourceTitle: Catechetical Instructions of St. John Vianney
notes: Recorded by witnesses from his catechetical instructions. Translated from French.
Frequently repeated counsel found in Padre Pio's correspondence.
